content: The two parabolas x2 = 4y and y2 = 4x meet in two distinct points. One of these is the origin and the other is
explanation:
Given equations of parabolas are

x2 = 4y ..... (i)

y2 = 4x ..... (ii)

or $${\left( {{{{y^2}} \over 4}} \right)^2} = 4y$$ (using (i))

$${{{y^4}} \over {16}} = 4y \Rightarrow {y^4} - 64y = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow y({y^3} - 64) = 0$$ either y = 0 or y = 4

Substituting y = 4, x = 4 $$\therefore$$ point of intersection is (4, 4).

content: The vertex of the parabola x2 + 2y = 8x $$-$$ 7 is
explanation:
Given equation of parabola is

$${x^2} + 2y = 8x - 7$$

or, $${x^2} - 8x + 2y + 7 = 0$$

or, $${x^2} - 8x + 16 + 2y + 7 - 16 = 0$$

or, $${(x - 4)^2} = - 2\left( {y - {9 \over 2}} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ Vertex $$\left( {4,{9 \over 2}} \right)$$.

content: If P(at2, 2at) be one end of a focal chord of the parabola y2 = 4ax, then the length of the chord is
explanation:
Given equation of parabola is $${y^2} = 4ax$$

Let $$P(a{t^2},2at)$$ and $$A(at_1^2,2a{t_1})$$ are two ends of focal chord

Since length of focal chord

$$ = a{({t_1} - t)^2} = a{\left( { - {1 \over t} - t} \right)^2}$$ ($$\because$$ $$t{t_1} = - 1$$)

$$ = a{\left( {t + {1 \over t}} \right)^2}$$.

content: The length of the common chord of the parabolas y2 = x and x2 = y is
explanation:
Given equations of parabolas are

y2 = x ...... (i)

x2 = y ...... (ii)

Substituting (ii) and (i)

x4 $$-$$ x = 0 $$\Rightarrow$$ x(x3 $$-$$ 1) = 0 $$\Rightarrow$$ either x = 0 or x = 1

Substituting x = 0, y = 0

Substituting x = 1, y = 1

$$\therefore$$ Points of intersections are (0, 0) and (1, 1)

$$\therefore$$ Length of common chord

$$ = \sqrt {{{(1 - 0)}^2} + {{(1 - 0)}^2}} = \sqrt {1 + 1} = \sqrt 2 $$.

content: The coordinates of the focus of the parabola described parametrically by x = 5t2 + 2, y = 10t + 4 are
explanation:
Equation of parabola in parametric form is

x = 5t2 + 2, y = 10t + 4.

Eliminating t we get (y $$-$$ 4)2 = 20(x $$-$$ 2)

Comparing with Y2 = 4AX

y $$-$$ 4 = Y ..... (i)

x $$-$$ 2 = X ..... (ii)

4A = 20 $$\Rightarrow$$ A = 5

Focus of Y2 = 4AX is (A, 0) = (5, 0)

$$\therefore$$ y $$-$$ 4 = 0, x $$-$$ 2 = 5 or y = 4, x = 7

$$\therefore$$ Coordinates of focus of parabola are (7, 4).

content: If t1 and t2 be the parameters of the end points of a focal chord for the parabola y2 = 4ax, then which one is true?
explanation:
The equation of the parabola is given by $y^2 = 4ax$, where $a$ is a constant. The focal chord of a parabola is a chord passing through the focus of the parabola.

The focus of the parabola is $(0, a/4)$.

Let $t_1$ and $t_2$ be the parameters of the end points of the focal chord. Then the end points of the chord are $(at_1^2, 2at_1)$ and $(at_2^2, 2at_2)$.

The slope of the chord is given by $(2at_2 - 2at_1)/(at_2^2 - at_1^2)$, which simplifies to $(t_2 + t_1)/a$.

The slope of the axis of the parabola is given by the derivative of $y^2 = 4ax$, which is $dy/dx = 2y/4a = y/2a$. At the vertex $(0, 0)$, the slope of the axis is 0.

The slope of the axis is perpendicular to the slope of the chord. Therefore,

$\frac{y}{2a}\frac{t_2 + t_1}{a} = -1$

Simplifying this equation, we get:

$t_1t_2 = -1$

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{\ t_1t_2 = -1}$.

content: The vertex of the parabola y2 + 6x $$-$$ 2y + 13 = 0 is
explanation:
$${y^2} - 2y = - 6x - 13 \Rightarrow {(y - 1)^2} = - 6(x + 2)$$

$$\therefore$$ Vertex is given by x + 2 = 0 & $$y - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow ( - 2,1)$$

content: The coordinates of a moving point P are (2t2 + 4, 4t + 6). Then its locus will be a/an
explanation:
x = 2t2 + 4, y = 4t + 6

$$ \Rightarrow t = {{y - 6} \over 4}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x = 2{\left( {{{y - 6} \over 4}} \right)^2} + 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(y - 6)^2} = 8(x - 4)$$, which is a parabola.

content: The locus of the middle points of all chords of the parabola y2 = 4ax passing through the vertex is
explanation:
y2 = 4ax ...... (i)

Let P(at2, 2at) be any point, on (i) and A be vertex of (i).

Let R(x, y) be midpoint of chord AP.

$$\therefore$$ $$x = {{a{t^2} + 0} \over 2}$$, $$y = {{2at + 0} \over 2} = at$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x = {a \over 2}.{\left( {{y \over a}} \right)^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} = 2ax$$ is the locus of the middle points of all chords of (i) passing through vertex.

content: The line $y-\sqrt{3} x+3=0$ cuts the parabola $y^2=x+2$ at the points $P$ and $Q$. If the co-ordinates of the point $X$ are $(\sqrt{3}, 0)$, then the value of $X P \cdot X Q$ is
explanation:
The line $y-\sqrt{3} x+3=0$ passes through $X(\sqrt{3}, 0)$ because $0-\sqrt{3}(\sqrt{3})+3=0$

The distance from $X(\sqrt{3}, 0)$ to any point $(x, y)$ on the line is

             $$ d=\sqrt{(x-\sqrt{3})^2+y^2} $$

Since,  $$ y=\sqrt{3}(x-\sqrt{3}) $$

we have$$ y^2=3(x-\sqrt{3})^2 $$

$$ \begin{aligned}So,\,\, d & =\sqrt{(x-\sqrt{3})^2+3(x-\sqrt{3})^2} \\ & =\sqrt{4(x-\sqrt{3})^2} \\ & =2|x-\sqrt{3}| \end{aligned} $$

The line intersects the parabola $y^2=x+2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Substitute } \quad y=\sqrt{3} x-3 \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} &(\sqrt{3} x-3)^2=x+2 \\ & 3 x^2-6 \sqrt{3} x+9=x+2 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x^2-(6 \sqrt{3}+1) x+7=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Let the roots of this quadratic be $x_P$ and $x_Q$

$$ \begin{array}{r} x_P \cdot x_Q=\left(2\left|x_P-\sqrt{3}\right|\right)\left(2\left|x_Q-\sqrt{3}\right|\right)=4 \\ \left|\left(x_P-\sqrt{3}\right)\left(x_Q-\sqrt{3}\right)\right|=4\left|x_P x_Q-\sqrt{3}\left(x_P+x_Q\right)+3\right| \end{array} $$

From vieta's formulas, $x_P+x_Q=\frac{6 \sqrt{3}+1}{3}$ and

$$ \begin{aligned} &x_P x_Q=\frac{7}{3} .\\ &\text { substitute these values }\\ &\begin{aligned} x_P \cdot x_Q & =4\left|\frac{7}{3}-\sqrt{3}\left(\frac{6 \sqrt{3}+1}{3}\right)+3\right| \\ & =4\left|\frac{7-(18+\sqrt{3})+9}{3}\right| \\ & =4\left|\frac{-2-\sqrt{3}}{3}\right|=4\left(\frac{2+\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: $$\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$$ is an equilateral triangle inscribed in the parabola $$\mathrm{y}^2=4 \mathrm{a} x, \mathrm{a}>0$$ with O as the vertex, then the length of the side of $$\triangle \mathrm{O A B}$$ is

Let A be the point (0, 4) in the xy-plane and let B be the point (2t, 0). Let L be the midpoint of AB and let the perpendicular bisector of AB meet the y-axis M. Let N be the midpoint of LM. Then locus of N is
explanation:
We have, $$A(0,4), B(2 t, 0)$$

Since, $$L$$ is mid-point of $$A B$$,

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \quad L\left(\frac{0+2 t}{2}, \frac{4+0}{2}\right) \text { i.e. } L(t, 2) \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & m=\text { Slope of } A B=\frac{0-4}{2 t-0}=\frac{-2}{t} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Slope of line perpendicular to $$A B=\frac{-1}{m_1}=\frac{t}{2}$$

So, equation of perpendicular bisector of $$A B$$ is

$$y-2=\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)(x-t)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Putting, } x=0, y-2=\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)(-t) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=2-\frac{t^2}{2} \end{aligned}$$

So, $$M\left(0,2-\frac{t^2}{2}\right)$$

As, $$N$$ is mid-point of $$L M$$,

So, $$N\left(\frac{t+0}{2}, \frac{2+2-\frac{t^2}{2}}{2}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { i.e., } N\left(\frac{t}{2}, 2-\frac{t^2}{4}\right) \\ & \text { Let } \frac{t}{2}=h, 2-\frac{t^2}{4}=k \\ & \Rightarrow k=2-\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)^2=2-h^2 \\ & \Rightarrow h^2+k-2=0 \Rightarrow h^2=2-k \end{aligned}$$

i.e., Locus of $$N$$ is $$x^2=2-y$$, which is a parabola.

content: Let O be the vertex, Q be any point on the parabola x$$^2$$ = 8y. If the point P divides the line segment OQ internally in the ratio 1 : 3, then the locus of P is :
explanation:
Vertex $$O$$, of parabola $$x^2=8 y$$ is $$O(0,0)$$

Let $$Q$$ be a point on parabola i.e., $$Q\left(4 t, 2 t^2\right), P(h, k)$$ divides $$O Q$$ internally in ratio $$1: 3$$.

Image

$$\therefore \quad h =\frac{1 \times 4 t+3 \times 0}{1+3}=t, k=\frac{1 \times 2 t^2+3 \times 0}{1+3}=\frac{t^2}{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad k =\frac{h^2}{2}$$

i.e., Locus of $$P$$ is $$x^2=2 y$$.

content: From the focus of the parabola $${y^2} = 12x$$, a ray of light is directed in a direction making an angle $${\tan ^{ - 1}}{3 \over 4}$$ with x-axis. Then the equation of the line along which the reflected ray leaves the parabola is
explanation:
$$\text { We have, equation of parabola i.e.. } y^2=12 x$$

Here, $$a=3$$

So, co-ordinate of focus is $$(3,0)$$

Let $$A$$ be the focus of parabola and $$A B$$ is the incident ray and $$B C$$ be the reflected ray.

Slope of $$A B=\tan \theta=\frac{3}{4}$$

Equation of $$A B$$ is

$$y-0=\frac{3}{4}(x-3)$$

$$4 y=3 x-9$$

Let co-ordinate of $$B$$ be $$\left(\frac{\alpha^2}{12}, \alpha\right)$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & 4 \alpha=\frac{3 \alpha^2}{12}-9 \\ & 16 \alpha=\alpha^2-36 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \alpha^2-16 \alpha-36=0 \\ & \alpha=18 \quad [\text{Neglecting}~\alpha=-2] \end{aligned}$$

The reflected ray leaves the parabola at $$y=18$$

content: The point of contact of the tangent to the parabola y2 = 9x which passes through the point (4, 10) and makes an angle $$\theta$$ with the positive side of the axis of the parabola where tan$$\theta$$ > 2, is
explanation:
Equation of the tangent at any point $$\left[ {{{9{t^2}} \over 4},{{9t} \over 2}} \right]$$ on the parabola is

$$ty = x + {{9{t^2}} \over 4}$$ and it passes through (4, 10)

$${y^2} = 9x$$

$$\therefore$$ $$a = {9 \over 4}$$

$$y = mx + {a \over m}$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = mx + {9 \over {4m}}$$ [$$\because$$ $$a = {9 \over 4}$$]

Since, it passes through (4, 10)

$$ \Rightarrow 10 = 4m + {9 \over {4m}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{m^2} - 40m + 9 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow m = {1 \over 4},{9 \over 4}$$

Equation of the tangents are

$$Y = {1 \over 4}x + 9$$ and $$y = {9 \over 4}x + 1$$

$$x - 4y + 36 = 0$$

$$9x - 4y + 4 = 0$$

$$y = mx + {a \over m}$$

touches at $$\left( {{a \over {{m^2}}},{{2a} \over m}} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ Point of contact are (36, 18) and $$\left[ {{4 \over 9},2} \right]$$.

content: A line passes through the point $$( - 1,1)$$ and makes an angle $${\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {{3 \over 5}} \right)$$ in the positive direction of x-axis. If this line meets the curve $${x^2} = 4y - 9$$ at A and B, then |AB| is equal to
explanation:
Let $$\theta = {\sin ^{ - 1}}{3 \over 5}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin \theta = {3 \over 5} \Rightarrow \tan \theta = {3 \over 4}$$

So, equation of the line passes through ($$-$$1, 1) with slope $${3 \over 4}$$ is $$y - 1 = \left( {{3 \over 4}} \right)(x + 1)$$

$$3x - 4y + 7 = 0$$

Which meets the curve $${x^2} = 4y - 9$$ at points for which $$3x - ({x^2} + 9) + 7 = 0$$ or $${x^2} - 3x + 2 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = 1,2$$ and the coordinates of A are $$\left( {1,{{10} \over 4}} \right)$$ and of B are $$\left( {2,{{13} \over 4}} \right)$$.

So, that $$|AB| = \sqrt {{{(2 - 1)}^2} + {{\left( {{{13} \over 4} - {{10} \over 4}} \right)}^2}} = {5 \over 4}$$ units

content: Let P be a point on (2, 0) and Q be a variable point on (y $$-$$ 6)2 = 2(x $$-$$ 4). Then the locus of mid-point of PQ is
explanation:
Let the coordinates of Q be (h, k)

and $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ be the mid-point of PQ.

Then,

$$\alpha = {{2 + h} \over 2}$$,

$$h = 2\alpha - 2$$

and $$\beta = {{0 + k} \over 2}$$

$$k = 2\beta $$

Since, $$Q(h,k)$$ lies on $${(y - 6)^2} = 2(x - 4)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(k - 6)^2} = 2(h - 4)$$ ..... (i)

Put the value of h and k in Eq. (i)

$${(2\beta - 6)^2} = 2(2\alpha - 2 - 4)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4{(\beta - 3)^2} = 2(2\alpha - 6)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{(\beta - 3)^2} = 2(\alpha - 3)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\beta ^2} + 9 - 6\beta = \alpha - 3$$

$$\alpha - {\beta ^2} + 6\beta - 12 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ The locus of $$(h,k)$$ is $$x - {y^2} + 6y - 12 = 0$$

content: AB is a chord of a parabola y2 = 4ax, (a > 0) with vertex A. BC is drawn perpendicular to AB meeting the axis at C. The projection of BC on the axis of the parabola is
explanation:
We know that,

In $$\Delta$$ABD, we have,

$$\tan \theta = {y \over x}$$ ..... (i)

In $$\Delta$$BCD, we have $$\tan (90^\circ - \theta ) = {y \over {CD}}$$

$$CD = y\tan \theta = {{{y^2}} \over x}$$ [by using Eq. (i)]

$$CD = {{4ax} \over x} = 4a$$ [$$\because$$ $${y^2} = 4ax$$]

content: From the point ($$-$$1, $$-$$6), two tangents are drawn to y2 = 4x. Then the angle between the two tangents is
explanation:
Equation of tangent to $${y^2} = 4x$$ is $$y = mx + {1 \over m}$$

Since, tangent passes through $$( - 1, - 6)$$

$$ - 6 = - m + {1 \over m}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {m^2} - 6m - 1 = 0$$

On solving above equation, we get

$${m_1} = 6.16$$

$${m_2} = - 0.16$$

$${m_1}{m_2} = 6.16 \times ( - 0.16)$$

$${m_1}{m_2} \cong - 1$$

$$\therefore$$ The angle between them is 90$$^\circ$$ (approx).

content: Let the tangent and normal at any point P(at2, 2at), (a > 0), on the parabola y2 = 4ax meet the axis of the parabola at T and G respectively. Then the radius of the circle through P, T and G is
explanation:
Tangent at P is $$ty = x + a{t^2}$$, which meets axis at $$T( - a{t^2},0)$$. Normal at P is $$tx + y = 2at + a{t^3}$$, which meets axis at $$G(2a + a{t^2},0)$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\angle TPG = {\pi \over 2}$$, so TG is diameter of the circle.

Equation of circle, is

$$(x + a{t^2})(x - 2a - a{t^2}) + (y - 0)(y - 0) = 0$$

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 2ax - a{t^2}(2a + a{t^2}) = 0$$

Here, $$g = - a$$

$$c = a{t^2}(2a + a{t^2})$$

$$f = 0$$

So, radius $$(r) = \sqrt {{g^2} + {f^2} - c} $$

$$ \Rightarrow r = \sqrt {{{( - a)}^2} + 0 + a{t^2}(2a + a{t^2})} $$

$$r = a\sqrt {1 + 2{t^2} + {t^4}} $$

$$ = a(1 + {t^2})$$

$$r = a(1 + {t^2})$$

content: If P1P2 and P3P4 are two focal chords of the parabola y2 = 4ax then the chords P1P3 and P2P4 intersect on the
explanation:
Let coordinates of P1 be $$(at_1^2,2a{t_1})$$, then coordinates of P2 are $$\left( {{a \over {t_1^2}},{{ - 2a} \over {{t_1}}}} \right)$$.

Similarly, if coordinates of P3 are $$(at_2^2,2a{t_2})$$.

Then, coordinates of P4 are $$\left( {{a \over {t_2^2}},{{ - 2a} \over {{t_2}}}} \right)$$.

An equation of the chord $${P_1}{P_3}$$ is

$${{x - at_1^2} \over {at_2^2 - at_1^2}} = {{y - 2a{t_1}} \over {2a{t_2} - 2a{t_1}}}$$

or $$2(x + a{t_1}{t_2}) = ({t_1} + {t_2})y$$ ...... (i)

Similarly, the equation of the chord $${P_2}{P_4}$$ is

$$2\left( {x + {a \over {{t_1}{t_2}}}} \right) = - \left( {{1 \over {{t_1}}} + {1 \over {{t_2}}}} \right)y$$

$$2({t_1}{t_2}x + a) = - ({t_1} + {t_2})y$$ ...... (ii)

Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$2(1 + {t_1}{t_2})(x + a) = 0$$

$$x + a = 0$$

Therefore, intersection of point $${P_1}{P_3}$$ and $${P_2}{P_4}$$ lies on directrix.

content: The locus of the vertices of the family of parabolas $$6y = 2{a^3}{x^2} + 3{a^2}x - 12a$$ is
explanation:
Given equation of parabola

$$6y = 2{a^3}{x^2} + 3{a^2}x - 12a$$

$$ \Rightarrow 6y + 12a = 2a\left( {{a^2}{x^2} + {3 \over 2}ax} \right) = 2a\left( {{a^2}{x^2} + 2\,.\,{3 \over 4}ax + {9 \over {16}}} \right) - {9 \over 8}a$$

$$ \Rightarrow 6y + {{105} \over 8}a = 2a{\left( {ax + {3 \over 4}} \right)^2}$$

Let vertices are (h, k)

$$\therefore$$ $$h = {{ - 3} \over {4a}}$$ and $$k = {{ - 35a} \over {16}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$hk = {{105} \over {64}}$$

$$\therefore$$ locus of the vertices is $$xy = {{105} \over {64}}$$

content: From a point (d, 0) three normal are drawn to the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = x, then
explanation:
Given, equation of parabola y2 = x

We know that for y2 = 4ax

if we draw a normal from (h, 0) on it then condition for three normal is h > 2a

$$\therefore$$ d > $${1 \over 2}$$

content: The length of the chord of the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 4ax(a > 0) which passes through the vertex and makes an acute angle $$\alpha $$ with the axis of the parabola is
explanation:
Given, parabola y2 = 4ax


Let the point A on parabola (at2, 2at) in $$\Delta $$OAB

$$\cot \,\alpha = {{a{t^2}} \over {2at}} = {t \over 2}$$

$$t = 2\,\cot \,\alpha $$

In $$\Delta OAB$$

$$\sin \,\alpha = {{AB} \over {OA}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow OA = AB\,\cos ec\,\alpha = 2at\,\cos ec\,\alpha $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$OA = 2a(2\,\cot \,\alpha )\,\cos ec\,\alpha $$

$$OA = 4a\,\cot \,\alpha \,\cos ec\,\alpha $$

$$ \therefore $$ Length of chord = $$4a\,\cot \,\alpha \,\cos ec\,\alpha $$

content: The equation of the latusrectum of a parabola is x + y = 8 and the equation of the tangent at the vertex is x + y = 12. Then, the length of the latusrectum is
explanation:
Given, equation of latusrectum of parabola is x + y = 8

and equation of tangent at vertex is x + y = 12

Since both are parallel.

$$ \therefore $$ The distance between latusrectum and equation of tangent at vertex is 'a'

$$ \therefore $$ $$a = {4 \over {\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2}} }} = {4 \over {\sqrt 2 }} = 2\sqrt 2 $$

$$ \therefore $$ Length of latusrectum = $$4a = 8\sqrt 2 $$ unit

content: If the line y = x is a tangent to the parabola y = ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + c at the point (1, 1) and the curve passes through ($$ - $$1, 0), then
explanation:
Given parabola

y = ax2 + bx + c

put x = 1, y = 1, we get

1 = a + b + c ......(i)

y = x is tangent of the parabola at (1, 1).

$$ \therefore $$ $${\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)_{(1,1)}} = 2ax + b$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 1 = 2a + b .......(ii)

Also curve passes through ($$ - $$1, 0)

$$ \therefore $$ 0 = a $$ - $$ b + c ........(iii)

From Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get

$$a = c = {1 \over 4}$$

and $$b = {1 \over 2}$$

content: Number of common tangents of y = x<sup>2</sup> and y = $$-$$x<sup>2</sup> + 4x $$-$$ 4 is
explanation:
We have,

equation of parabola y = x2

Let P($$\alpha$$ , $$\alpha$$2) is a point on the parabola.

$$ \therefore $$ $$y - {\alpha ^2} = 2\alpha (x - \alpha )$$

$$ \because $$ $$\left[ {{{dy} \over {dx}} = 2x \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {d{x_{(\alpha ,{\alpha ^2})}}}} = 2\alpha } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = 2\alpha x - {\alpha ^2}$$

Also, given $$y = - {x^2} + 4x - 4$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$ - {x^2} + 4x - 4 = 2\alpha x - {\alpha ^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + 2x(\alpha - 2) + (4 - {\alpha ^2}) = 0$$

Discriminant = 0

$$4{(\alpha - 2)^2} - 4(4 - {\alpha ^2}) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(\alpha - 2)^2} - (4 - {\alpha ^2}) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2} - 4\alpha + 4 - 4 + {\alpha ^2} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2} - 2\alpha = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 0,\,\alpha = 2$$

content: Let P(at<sup>2</sup>, 2at), Q, R(ar<sup>2</sup>, 2ar) be three points on a parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 4ax. If PQ is the focal chord and PK, QR are parallel where the co-ordinates of K is (2a, 0), then the value of r is
explanation:
Here, coordinate of Q will be $$\left( {{a \over {{t_2}}},{{2a} \over t}} \right)$$

Slope of QR = $${2 \over {r - {1 \over t}}}$$

$$PK = {{2at} \over {a{t^2} - 2a}} = {{2t} \over {{t^2} - 2}}$$

Since, slope of QR = Slope of PK

$$ \therefore $$ $${2 \over {r - {1 \over t}}} = {{2t} \over {{t^2} - 2}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${r = {{{t^2} - 1} \over t}}$$

content: Consider the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 4x. Let P and Q be points on the parabola where P(4, $$-$$ 4) and Q(9, 6). Let R be a point on the arc of the parabola between P and Q. Then, the area of $$\Delta$$PQR is largest when
explanation:
Equation of PQ is 2x $$-$$ y = 12


Perpendicular distance

$$AR = \left| {{{2{t^2} - 2t - 12} \over {\sqrt 5 }}} \right| = {2 \over {\sqrt 5 }}(t - 3)(t + 2)$$

AR is Maximum, at $$t = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ R is $$\left( {{1 \over 4},1} \right)$$

content: The axis of the parabola $${x^2} + 2xy + {y^2} - 5x + 5y - 5 = 0$$ is
explanation:
Given, equation, $${x^2} + 2xy + {y^2} - 5x + 5y - 5 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(x + y)^2} = 5x - 5y + 5$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(x + y)^2} = 5(x - y + 1)$$

$$ \therefore $$ Axis of the parabola is x + y = 0

content: A line passing through the point of intersection of x + y = 4 and x $$-$$ y = 2 makes an angle $${\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{3 \over 4}} \right)$$ with the X-axis. It intersects the parabola $${y^2} = 4(x - 3)$$ at points $$({x_1},{y_1})$$ and $$({x_2},{y_2})$$, respectively. Then, $$|{x_1},{y_2}|$$ is equal to
explanation:
Given equations are

x + y = 4 .... (i)

and x $$-$$ y = 2 .... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

x = 3 and y = 1

The line through this point making an angle $${\tan ^{ - 1}}{3 \over 4}$$ with the X-axis is

$$(y - 1) = {3 \over 4}(x - 3)$$ $$ \because $$ $$\left[ {m = {3 \over 4}} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = {{3x} \over 4} - {5 \over 4} = {{3x - 5} \over 4}$$ ... (iii)

Since, this line intersects the parabola $${y^2} = 4(x - 3)$$ at points $$({x_1},{y_1})$$ and $$({x_2},{y_2})$$ respectively.

$$ \therefore $$ Putting $$y = {{3x - 5} \over 4}$$ in equation of parabola, we get

$${\left( {{{3x - 5} \over 4}} \right)^2} = 4(x - 3)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 9{x^2} - 94x + 217 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x_1} + {x_2} = {{94} \over 9}$$ and $${x_1}{x_2} = {{217} \over 9}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$|{x_1} - {x_2}|\, = \,\sqrt {{{({x_1} + {x_2})}^2} - 4{x_1}{x_2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{{\left( {{{94} \over 9}} \right)}^2} - 4 \times {{217} \over 9}} $$

$$ = {{32} \over 9}$$

content: If the vertex of the conic $${y^2} - 4y = 4x - 4a$$ always lies between the straight lines $$x + y = 3$$ and $$2x + 2y - 1 = 0$$, then
explanation:
Equation of the vertex of conic

$${y^2} - 4y = 4x - 4a$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} - 4y + 4 = 4x - 4a + 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(y - 2)^2} = 4[x - (a - 1)]$$

$$ \therefore $$ Vertex = (a $$-$$1, 2)



From figure,

clearly, $$ - {3 \over 2} < a - 1 < 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$ - {1 \over 2} < a < 2$$

content: The locus of the mid-points of all chords of the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 4ax through its vertex is another parabola with directrix
explanation:
Let P (at2, 2at) be a moving point on the parabola y2 = 4ax and S(a, 0) be its focus. Let Q(h, k) be the mid-point of SP.

Then, $$h = {{a{t^2} + a} \over 2}$$

and $$k = {{2at + 0} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{2h} \over a} = {t^2} + 1$$

and $$t = {k \over a}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{2h} \over a} = {{{k^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {k^2} = 2ah - {a^2}$$ [on eliminating t]

Thus, the locus of (h, k) is $${y^2} = 2ax - {a^2}$$

Now, $${y^2} = 2ax - {a^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(y - 0)^2} = 2a\left( {x - {a \over 2}} \right)$$

The equation of the directrix of this parabola is

$$x - {a \over 2} = - {a \over 2}$$, i.e. x = 0

content: If the tangent to the parabola y = x(2 $$-$$ x) at the point (1, 1) intersects the parabola at P. Find the co-ordinate of P.
explanation:
$$\therefore$$ $$y = x(2 - x)$$ ..... (i)

$$\therefore$$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} = {d \over {dx}}(2x - {x^2}) = 2 - 2x$$

Slope of tangent at (1, 1) is

$${\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)_{(1,1)}} = 2 - 2 \times 1 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ equation of tangent at (1, 1) is

$$y - {y_1} = {\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)_{({x_1},{y_1})}}(x - {x_1})$$

$$ \Rightarrow y - 1 = 0(x - 1) \Rightarrow y = 1$$ ..... (ii)

To find the co-ordinates of P, we solve (i) and (ii)

$$\therefore$$ $$2x - {x^2} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 2x + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(x - 1)^2} = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ x = 1, 1

$$\because$$ both values of x are same.

$$\therefore$$ both points coincide. So, co-ordinates of P are (1, 1), i.e. given point (1, 1) is point P.

Note : If tangent is drawn at any point on parabola, it will never intersect the parabola again at other point.

So, given point (1, 1) is the point P.

content: Prove that for all values of m, except zero the st. line $$y = mx + {a \over m}$$ touches the parabola y2 = 4ax
explanation:
Find the point of intersection of

$$y = mx + {a \over m}$$ and $${y^2} = 4ax$$

$$\therefore$$ $$y = {{4a} \over m}\left( {y - {a \over m}} \right) \Rightarrow {y^2} - {{4a} \over m}y + {{4{a^2}} \over {{m^2}}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {y - {{2a} \over m}} \right)^2} = 0 \Rightarrow y = {{2a} \over m},{{2a} \over m}$$

$$\because$$ both values of y are same, so there is only one point of intersection. But in case of parabola y2 = 4ax if line intersects at one point whose slope is not zero, then the line touches the parabola. So, $$y = mx + {a \over m}$$ touches the parabola y2 = 4ax.

content: Find the angle subtended by the double ordinate of length 2a of the parabola y2 = ax at its vertex.
explanation:
Let AB be the double ordinate and $$\angle$$AOB be the angle made by AB at the origin.

AB = 2a (given)

co-ordinates of A $$\equiv$$ (a, a)

co-ordinates of B $$\equiv$$ (a, $$-$$a)

co-ordinates of O $$\equiv$$ (0, 0)

Slope of OA(m1) = $${{0 - a} \over {0 - a}} = 1$$

Slope of OB(m2) = $${{0 - a} \over {0 + a}} = -1$$

Angle between OA and OB

$$\tan \theta = \left| {{{{m_2} - {m_1}} \over {1 + {m_1}{m_2}}}} \right| = \left| {{{ - 1 - 0} \over {1 + ( - 1)}}} \right| = {1 \over 0}$$ = Not defined

$$0 = {\tan ^{ - 1}}(\infty ) \Rightarrow \theta = \pi /2$$.

$$\angle$$AOB = $$\pi$$/2.

content: Let A, B the two distinct points on the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 4x. If the axis of the parabola touches a circle of radius r having AB as diameter, the slope of the line AB is
explanation:
Centre of circle = $$\left( {{{t_1^2 + t_2^2} \over 2},({t_1} + {t_2})} \right)$$


Since, circle touch the x-axis, so equation of tangent is y = 0

$$\because$$ Radius = Perpendicular distance from centre to the tangent

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Radius = |t1 + t2| = r

Slope of AB = $${2 \over {{t_1} + {t_2}}} = {2 \over { \pm r}}$$

content: The area of the region lying above X-axis, and included between the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 2ax and the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = ax, a > 0 is
explanation:
Given, equation of circle

$${x^2} + {y^2} = 2ax$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(x - a)^2} + {y^2} = {a^2}$$

and equation of parabola is $${y^2} = ax,a > 0$$


Intersection points of circle and parabola

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + ax = 2ax$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} = ax$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - ax = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x(x - a) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = 0,a$$

Intersecting points are (0, 0) and (a, a).

$$ \therefore $$ Required area = $${{\pi {a^2}} \over 4} - \int_0^a {\sqrt {ax} dx} $$

$$ = {{\pi {a^2}} \over 4} - \sqrt a \left( {{{{x^{3/2}}} \over {3/2}}} \right)_0^a$$

$$ = {{\pi {a^2}} \over 4} - {{2{a^2}} \over 3} = {a^2}\left( {{\pi \over 4} - {2 \over 3}} \right)$$

content: If the tangent to $${y^2} = 4ax$$ at the point $$(a{t^2},2at)$$ where | t | > 1 is a normal to $${x^2} - {y^2} = {a^2}$$ at the point $$(a\sec \theta ,a\tan \theta )$$, then
explanation:
Equation of tangent to $${y^2} = 4ax$$ at $$(a{t^2},2at)$$ will be

$$x - yt = - a{t^2}$$ ..... (i)

Also, equation of normal to $${x^2} - {y^2} = {a^2}$$ at $$(a\sec \theta ,a\tan \theta )$$ will be

$${x \over {a\sec \theta }} + {y \over {a\tan \theta }} = 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow x + y\cos ec = 2a\sec \theta $$ ..... (ii)

Since, Eqs. (i) and (ii) are identical.

$$ \therefore $$ $$t = - \cos ec\theta $$ or $$t = 2\tan \theta $$

content: The focus of the conic x<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 6x + 4y + 1 = 0 is
explanation:
We have,

x2 $$-$$6x + 4y + 1 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ (x $$-$$ 3)2 $$-$$ 9 + 4y + 1 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ (x $$-$$ 3)2 + 4y $$-$$ 8 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ (x $$-$$ 3)2 = $$-$$ 4(y $$-$$ 2)

It represents parabola whose vertex is (3, 2)

$$ \therefore $$ Focus = (3, $$-$$1 + 2) = (3, 1)
